primary_diagnosis.name: Acute sacroiliac / paraspinal inflammatory muscle spasm — flare of known non-radiographic axial spondyloarthritis (HLA-B27 positive)

pathophysiology_summary: The patient has known non-radiographic axial spondyloarthritis (nr-axSpA): HLA-B27-associated immune-mediated inflammation of the sacroiliac joints and axial entheses that is active on MRI/clinically but not yet visible on plain radiographs. The classic hallmark is inflammatory back pain — stiffness that is WORSE with rest and BETTER with movement. Today the patient sustained an acute mechanical/inflammatory paraspinal and sacroiliac muscle spasm precipitated by prolonged static sitting followed by attempted standing; the underlying enthesitis lowers the threshold for painful protective muscle 'splinting'. This is a benign (non-surgical) flare. Its clinical danger is not the flare itself but that its dramatic, immobilising presentation MIMICS three time-critical emergencies that must be actively excluded: cauda equina syndrome, acute lumbar disc herniation with radiculopathy, and vertebral (osteoporotic) compression fracture. In this case each was excluded — on history (no sphincter disturbance, no saddle anaesthesia, no leg weakness, no radicular radiation, no trauma) and, in the source, on physical examination (normal power/reflexes, negative straight-leg-raise, no midline point tenderness). In telehealth the examination limb cannot be performed, so the history red-flag screen plus same-day in-person assessment carry the safety.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden severe low back pain with inability to mobilise mandates active exclusion of cauda equina before anything else — missing it causes irreversible sphincter and lower-limb dysfunction.

differential.diagnosis: Acute lumbar disc herniation with radiculopathy
differential.evidence_basis: A common cause of acute severe back pain with sudden mechanical onset; must be distinguished by the presence/absence of true radicular (dermatomal, below-knee) leg pain.
Considered for any acute severe axial pain; less likely without trauma or osteoporosis risk but not excludable on presentation alone.

differential.diagnosis: Acute flare of known axial spondyloarthritis with mechanical muscle spasm
differential.evidence_basis: Known HLA-B27 inflammatory back disease, classic morning-stiffness-improving-with-movement pattern, bilateral SI/lumbar distribution, mechanical precipitant — the most probable cause once red flags are cleared.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the leading probability is a benign flare, but the three emergencies are important-not-to-miss and MUST be screened before management. Anchoring on 'just a flare' without screening is the primary trap.

differential.diagnosis: Acute flare of known axial spondyloarthritis with sacroiliac / paraspinal muscle spasm
differential.evidence_basis: History red flags all negative (no sphincter disturbance, no saddle anaesthesia, no leg weakness, no radicular radiation, no trauma) — the benign inflammatory/mechanical flare fits the whole picture.

Bowel/bladder function intact, saddle sensation intact, no leg weakness on history. NOTE: telehealth cannot examine — exclusion is presumptive on history and REQUIRES same-day in-person assessment plus explicit safety-netting.

differential.diagnosis: Acute lumbar disc herniation with radiculopathy
differential.evidence_basis: No true radicular below-knee pain and bilateral non-dermatomal distribution argue against it, but it cannot be fully excluded without examination.
No trauma and no osteoporosis history make it unlikely; imaging would settle it if pain fails to improve.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After a negative history red-flag screen the probability strongly favours a benign flare, but because telehealth cannot perform the neurological examination that would confirm exclusion, the emergencies move to 'unlikely but not examined' — driving same-day in-person review, not discharge.

differential.diagnosis: Acute sacroiliac / paraspinal inflammatory muscle spasm — flare of known non-radiographic axial spondyloarthritis
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed in the source by normal neurological examination, negative straight-leg-raise and absence of point tenderness; benign, non-surgical flare managed with anti-inflammatories and graded mobilisation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final in-person diagnosis is a benign flare. The telehealth-correct endpoint is a presumptive flare diagnosis WITH same-day in-person confirmation of the excluded emergencies — not a remote 'all clear'.

red_flags.description: Bowel or bladder dysfunction (incontinence, retention, loss of control) — cardinal feature of cauda equina syndrome.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Presence would indicate cauda equina compression requiring emergency MRI and surgical decompression within hours.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Undetected cauda equina progresses to permanent incontinence, saddle anaesthesia, lower-limb weakness and sexual dysfunction.

red_flags.description: Saddle (perineal / perianal / genital) numbness or altered sensation — cardinal feature of cauda equina syndrome.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Saddle anaesthesia is a hard indication for emergency spinal imaging and neurosurgical referral.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delay to decompression causes irreversible neurological deficit.

red_flags.description: New, progressive or bilateral lower-limb weakness / legs giving way — cord or cauda equina compression.
red_flags.clinical_significance: True motor weakness (as opposed to pain-limited movement) signals neurological compromise needing urgent imaging.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to paraparesis if a compressive lesion is missed.

red_flags.description: Features of vertebral fragility fracture — significant trauma, osteoporosis, or midline spinous point tenderness.
red_flags.clinical_significance: A compression fracture changes management (imaging, bone-health work-up, analgesia) and can rarely be unstable.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed fracture leads to progressive deformity and untreated osteoporosis.

red_flags.description: True radicular pain radiating below the knee with dermatomal sensory loss — lumbar disc herniation with radiculopathy.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Radiculopathy alters management and, with progressive deficit, can become urgent.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Persistent or progressive nerve-root compression with sensorimotor deficit.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing the severe presentation to the known spondyloarthritis without screening for cauda equina, radiculopathy or fracture — premature closure that risks missing a surgical emergency.

Being swept up in the visceral severity and the patient's fear of a 'slipped disc' and dispatching a 000 ambulance / ED transfer for a flare with no red flags — the source explicitly judged an emergency transfer unnecessary and difficult.

Treating a negative history red-flag screen as equivalent to a normal neurological examination and discharging the patient, when telehealth cannot examine power, reflexes or straight-leg-raise — same-day in-person assessment is still required.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Rest advice for inflammatory back pain
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Advising bed rest and immobilisation, which worsens inflammatory back pain — the correct advice is graded, frequent, gentle movement plus anti-inflammatories.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Lower-limb motor power and reflexes (source: normal 5/5 power, intact reflexes bilaterally)
Straight-leg-raise testing (source: negative for radicular pain)
Perineal/saddle sensation by examination (source: grossly intact)
Paraspinal muscle spasm and sacroiliac joint tenderness on palpation (source: marked bilateral spasm, exquisite SI tenderness)
Midline spinous point tenderness for fracture
Blood pressure, heart rate and other clinician-measured vitals
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Screen the history red flags the patient CAN report (sphincter function, saddle sensation, leg weakness, radicular radiation, trauma). With all negative, arrange SAME-DAY in-person assessment (GP home/urgent-care review) to perform the neurological examination that cannot be done remotely, provide anti-inflammatory analgesia advice, and safety-net explicitly for cauda equina. Do NOT self-manage to a routine 1–2 week review, and do NOT dispatch a 000 ambulance.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate: the benign flare is the most likely answer, but a remote 'all clear' without the neurological examination could miss an early cauda equina or radiculopathy. The dominant risks are BOTH directions — anchoring/under-triage and fear-driven over-triage.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ASAS-EULAR recommendations for the management of axial spondyloarthritis (2022)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: NSAIDs are first-line for symptomatic axial spondyloarthritis; continuous vs on-demand use is individualised; physical activity and exercise are core.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ACSQHC Low Back Pain Clinical Care Standard / NICE NG59
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Assess for red flags (cauda equina, fracture, malignancy, infection); avoid routine imaging in non-specific low back pain; encourage staying active and discourage bed rest.
